analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 9 finding(s) from 10 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: DMI read requests can reach the debug module without a persistent successful JTAG/HMAC unlock when we_flag is deasserted; only writes appear to require the transient pass_check condition.
findings.vulnerability_category: issue read/write DMI transactions and trigger debug actions / debug module DMI request path and debug capabilities: debug_req/debug_resp to dm_top, debug_req_o, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG requester through tck_i/tms_i/td_i Operation: issue read/write DMI transactions and trigger debug actions Object: debug module DMI request path and debug capabilities: debug_req/debug_resp to dm_top, debug_req_o, ndmreset_o, dmactive_o Expected guard: DMI requests reaching debug functionality should be gated by persistent successful authentication/unlock state such as jtag_unlock/pass_check/hashValid, not only TAP DMIACCESS/update_dr selection. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag_tap can select DMIACCESS by IR value; dmi_jtag captures address/data on dmi_access && update_dr and permits READ transition when (pass_check | ~we_flag == 1). pass_check is defaulted low in combinational logic and jtag_unlock_o is assigned to pass_check, so it is not shown as a persistent unlock state. The request valid is forwarded through dmi_cdc to riscv_peripherals debug_req_valid/debug_req and dm_top. Path: JTAG pins -> riscv_peripherals.i_dmi_jtag -> dmi_jtag_tap DMIACCESS/update_dr -> dmi_jtag Read/Write -> dmi_cdc -> riscv_peripherals.debug_req/debug_req_valid -> dm_top DMI input Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A locked JTAG requester may issue DMI reads when we_flag_4 is deasserted, reaching debug-module DMI logic without successful authentication, potentially exposing or influencing debug state depending on dm_top semantics.

findings.summary: fuse_mem exposes security-sensitive fuse contents, including JTAG authentication hash and other key material, through a generic rdata_o path without internal privilege, lock, or address filtering.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/expose authentication hash or fuse words / jtag_hash / authentication fuse memory contents and other fuse-resident key material / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software/MMIO/NoC requester reaching fuse_mem through pkt_wrapper fuse_req/fuse_addr Operation: read/expose authentication hash or fuse words Object: jtag_hash / authentication fuse memory contents and other fuse-resident key material Expected guard: Fuse/hash contents used for debug authentication should be non-readable or readable only through explicit privilege/access-control/lock mediation with sensitive ranges filtered. Observed behavior: fuse_mem stores the JTAG expected HMAC hash in the same constant mem array used for rdata_o. On req_i, addr_q captures addr_i; rdata_o returns mem[addr_q] when in range. No privilege, lock, requester, or address-range filtering exists inside fuse_mem. Top-level connects pkt_wrapper fuse_req/fuse_addr/fuse_rdata to fuse_mem and also routes jtag_hash_o/ikey_hash_o/okey_hash_o into dmi_jtag. Path: NoC pkt port -> noc_axilite_bridge -> pkt_wrapper -> fuse_req/fuse_addr -> fuse_mem.rdata_o; fuse_mem.jtag_hash_o -> riscv_peripherals.jtag_hash -> dmi_jtag.jtag_hash_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: If pkt_wrapper exposes fuse_req/fuse_addr to MMIO/NoC requesters without adequate filtering, attackers can read debug authentication hash and other keys, undermining debug authentication and crypto secrecy.

findings.summary: Top-level declares acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl, derives acc_ctrl_c from acc_ctrl, passes acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][idx] and reglk_ctrl slices into many peripheral wrappers, and instantiates reglk_wrapper to drive reglk_ctrl. reglk_wrapper receives its own reglk slice, acct_ctrl, and jtag_unlock. The actual write update logic for reglk_ctrl/acc_ctrl and sticky lock behavior resides in wrapper modules not included in the provided evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure or overwrite access-control/lock policy / reglk_ctrl and acc_ctrl policy registers plus protected peripheral wrapper controls / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible register interface / NoC requester Operation: configure or overwrite access-control/lock policy Object: reglk_ctrl and acc_ctrl policy registers plus protected peripheral wrapper controls Expected guard: Writes to acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl should require authorized privilege and sticky lock enforcement; once locked, protected configuration/peripheral writes should be blocked. Observed behavior: Top-level declares acc_ctrl/reglk_ctrl, derives acc_ctrl_c from acc_ctrl, passes acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][idx] and reglk_ctrl slices into many peripheral wrappers, and instantiates reglk_wrapper to drive reglk_ctrl. reglk_wrapper receives its own reglk slice, acct_ctrl, and jtag_unlock. The actual write update logic for reglk_ctrl/acc_ctrl and sticky lock behavior resides in wrapper modules not included in the provided evidence. Path: NoC reglk/acct ports -> noc_axilite_bridge -> reglk_wrapper/acct_wrapper -> reglk_ctrl/acc_ctrl -> per-wrapper reglk_ctrl_i/acct_ctrl_i Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If wrappers do not enforce sticky locks and privilege checks, software/NoC requesters may rewrite policy or disable protections; visible top-level shows intended policy distribution but not enough evidence for a violation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Wrapper RTL for reglk_wrapper/acct_wrapper/peripheral wrappers is needed to verify write guards, sticky behavior, and whether locks can be cleared.

findings.summary: Top-level exposes many independent NoC AXI-lite bridge ports and passes acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][idx] plus reglk_ctrl slices into individual wrappers. Boot ROM explicitly gates rom_req with acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. DMA, pkt, reglk and other wrappers receive acct_ctrl_i. However, full address decode/range behavior and denied-response behavior are inside bridges/wrappers not provided; no complete overlap/default-route proof is available.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write peripheral registers or route transactions / security-sensitive peripheral MMIO regions and NoC response paths (AES, RSA, RNG, SHA256, HMAC, debug, reset, DMA, fuse/reglk/acct) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: network/MMIO requester on buf_ariane_*_noc2 inputs Operation: read/write peripheral registers or route transactions Object: security-sensitive peripheral MMIO regions and NoC response paths (AES, RSA, RNG, SHA256, HMAC, debug, reset, DMA, fuse/reglk/acct) Expected guard: Access to security-sensitive peripheral regions should be gated by access-control matrix and requester privilege/attributes; decode overlaps or default routes must not bypass acc_ctrl/reglk policy. Observed behavior: Top-level exposes many independent NoC AXI-lite bridge ports and passes acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][idx] plus reglk_ctrl slices into individual wrappers. Boot ROM explicitly gates rom_req with acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]. DMA, pkt, reglk and other wrappers receive acct_ctrl_i. However, full address decode/range behavior and denied-response behavior are inside bridges/wrappers not provided; no complete overlap/default-route proof is available. Path: buf_ariane_*_noc2 inputs -> per-peripheral noc_axilite_bridge -> wrapper AXI req/resp -> peripheral-specific request/response logic Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Potential unauthorized access through a wrapper or bridge cannot be ruled in or out from the visible evidence; top-level indicates intended per-wrapper gating but not complete range mediation.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need wrapper/bridge decode implementations and response mux/default behavior to determine whether access-control is enforced consistently.

findings.summary: TAP DMI scan selection and TDO output are not gated by debug authentication/unlock; testmode changes the TCK clock mux but normal shifting of DMI response data to TDO is available whenever the TAP selects DMIACCESS and enters shift states.
findings.vulnerability_category: shift/capture/update DMI data or select DMI chain in test mode / DMI scan chain / TDO output / debug request path / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: test/JTAG requester using testmode_i and TAP pins Operation: shift/capture/update DMI data or select DMI chain in test mode Object: DMI scan chain / TDO output / debug request path Expected guard: Test mode or TAP operation should not enable unauthorized DMI access or expose DMI response data on TDO unless normal debug authorization/unlock and TAP state checks are satisfied. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag_tap selects tdo_mux = dmi_tdo_i when jtag_ir_q is DMIACCESS and drives tdo_oe_o when shift_ir or shift_dr_o. dmi_access_o is an IR-selection output from TAP logic, with no evidence of jtag_unlock/pass_check gating in the TAP. testmode_i only selects between inverted and non-inverted TCK via a DFT clock mux, but the DMIACCESS TDO path and output enable are not auth-gated. Path: JTAG TAP testmode_i/DFT clock mux -> TAP state shift_dr_o -> DMIACCESS tdo_mux(dmi_tdo_i) -> td_o/tdo_oe_o; DMIACCESS/update_dr -> dmi_jtag request FSM Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An unauthenticated JTAG/test requester can select DMIACCESS and observe DMI scan-chain bits/TDO activity; combined with the DMI read gating issue, this can expose debug response data while locked.

findings.summary: The unlock output is tied to a transient combinational pass_check rather than a persistent authenticated state, and DMI reads can be allowed by ~we_flag without pass_check; the observed design does not ensure all debug access follows HMAC-valid comparison.
findings.vulnerability_category: authenticate/unlock debug access / jtag_unlock_o and debug-authentication state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external JTAG authentication requester Operation: authenticate/unlock debug access Object: jtag_unlock_o and debug-authentication state Expected guard: Unlock should be granted only after HMAC computation is complete and digest equals provisioned jtag_hash_i; bypass modes and pass_mode should not allow unconditional or stale unlock. Observed behavior: dmi_jtag contains HMAC-related signals and instance. Provided observation indicates jtag_unlock_o is assigned pass_check; pass_check is defaulted low in always_comb and only asserted transiently in PassChkValid after hashValid/pass_hash comparison. The request FSM allows READ when pass_check is true or we_flag is false. HMAC exposes hash_valid_o/ready_o but persistence and stale digest clearing are only partly visible. Path: dmi_jtag pass_data/startHash/pass_mode -> hmac instance -> pass_hash/hashValid/hmac_ready -> pass_check -> jtag_unlock_o and DMI request FSM Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Authentication state may be transient and not a robust session lock; more critically, reads may bypass HMAC when we_flag is deasserted. This weakens the intended JTAG password/HMAC protection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full dmi_jtag lines 90-199 and 318-334 were partly truncated; exact comparison expression and pass_data loading are from provided model observation rather than full raw snippet.

findings.summary: Top-level declares many rst_* signals and passes rst_i into wrappers. dmi_jtag assigns dmi_rst_no = rst_ni, and top-level leaves dmi_rst_no unconnected. dmi_jtag_tap includes DTMCS dmireset/dmihardreset fields and dmi_reset_o used to clear sticky DMI error state. ndmreset_o/dmactive_o/debug_req_o are outputs from dm_top, but their authorization semantics are inside dm_top, not provided. Reset-control wrapper decode for rst_* is not present in the visible evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: assert reset or clear state / reset control signals rst_1..rst_13, ndmreset_o, dmi_rst_no and security state affected by reset / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible reset interface and/or JTAG debug interface Operation: assert reset or clear state Object: reset control signals rst_1..rst_13, ndmreset_o, dmi_rst_no and security state affected by reset Expected guard: Reset/lifecycle operations affecting harts/peripherals/debug should require privilege/access-control or debug authorization, and reset should clear sensitive auth/session state safely without clearing sticky protections unexpectedly. Observed behavior: Top-level declares many rst_* signals and passes rst_i into wrappers. dmi_jtag assigns dmi_rst_no = rst_ni, and top-level leaves dmi_rst_no unconnected. dmi_jtag_tap includes DTMCS dmireset/dmihardreset fields and dmi_reset_o used to clear sticky DMI error state. ndmreset_o/dmactive_o/debug_req_o are outputs from dm_top, but their authorization semantics are inside dm_top, not provided. Reset-control wrapper decode for rst_* is not present in the visible evidence. Path: reset NoC/MMIO path and debug DMI reset fields -> wrapper rst_* outputs and dm_top ndmreset/debug controls; dmi_jtag DTMCS reset -> dmi_reset error clear Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: Unauthorized reset/lifecycle manipulation cannot be confirmed or dismissed; critical decode and security-state reset behavior resides in unprovided modules.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need reset wrapper/decode and dm_top behavior to verify guards for rst_* and ndmreset_o.

findings.summary: Top-level instantiates dma_wrapper with reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i(acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]), pmpcfg_i/pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and AXI req/resp from a DMA NoC AXI-lite bridge. This indicates intended controls are supplied. The actual DMA configuration registers, transfer-time checks, target address filtering, and race behavior are in dma_wrapper, not provided.
findings.vulnerability_category: configure DMA or perform DMA read/write transfer / DMA registers/transactions and protected memory/peripheral regions / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: DMA engine or software configuring DMA via NoC/MMIO Operation: configure DMA or perform DMA read/write transfer Object: DMA registers/transactions and protected memory/peripheral regions Expected guard: DMA configuration and active transfers should be restricted by privilege/access-control and range/PMP policy at configuration and use time. Observed behavior: Top-level instantiates dma_wrapper with reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i(acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][8]), pmpcfg_i/pmpaddr_i, we_flag_3, and AXI req/resp from a DMA NoC AXI-lite bridge. This indicates intended controls are supplied. The actual DMA configuration registers, transfer-time checks, target address filtering, and race behavior are in dma_wrapper, not provided. Path: buf_ariane_dma_noc2 -> noc_axilite_bridge -> dma_axi_req/resp -> dma_wrapper with acc_ctrl/reglk/PMP inputs Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: No direct top-level bypass is proven, but DMA access-control correctness cannot be established without dma_wrapper internals.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need dma_wrapper internals to inspect config-time and transfer-time access checks and target ranges.

findings.summary: hmac module computes ipad/opad from key_i and produces hash_o/ready/hash_valid. dmi_jtag instantiates hmac for authentication. Provided evidence does not show hash_o connected to top-level MMIO/TDO; dmi_jtag TDO path shifts DMI register data, not directly hmac hash_o in the visible snippets. However full dmi_jtag dataflow around pass_hash/pass_data and any readback of authentication registers was truncated, so exposure cannot be fully ruled out.
findings.vulnerability_category: compute or expose HMAC/hash state / HMAC key/input-derived internal state and hash output used for authentication / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: external observer through JTAG/TDO or software-visible interface Operation: compute or expose HMAC/hash state Object: HMAC key/input-derived internal state and hash output used for authentication Expected guard: HMAC key material/internal state should not be directly exposed on TDO/MMIO; hash_o should only feed intended comparison/authentication unless authorized. Observed behavior: hmac module computes ipad/opad from key_i and produces hash_o/ready/hash_valid. dmi_jtag instantiates hmac for authentication. Provided evidence does not show hash_o connected to top-level MMIO/TDO; dmi_jtag TDO path shifts DMI register data, not directly hmac hash_o in the visible snippets. However full dmi_jtag dataflow around pass_hash/pass_data and any readback of authentication registers was truncated, so exposure cannot be fully ruled out. Path: dmi_jtag HMAC instance key/hash inputs from fuse_mem -> hmac hash_o/pass_hash -> dmi_jtag comparison/control; possible but unproven path to DMI scan data/TDO Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: No direct secret-output exposure is proven from provided snippets; stale digest/bypass implications require full dmi_jtag consumer inspection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need complete dmi_jtag HMAC instance connections and DMI register/shift data assignments to determine whether pass_hash/hash_o can be read through DMI/TDO.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
